message.content: Multi-Factor Authentication Interception involves adversaries targeting MFA mechanisms (e.g., smart cards, token generators) to gain unauthorized access to systems and network resources. Despite MFA's enhanced security over traditional passwords, attackers can intercept and bypass these mechanisms. Common methods include using keyloggers to capture passwords associated with smart cards or other hardware tokens, replaying one-time passcodes, and predicting future authentication values by accessing algorithms and seed values. Adversaries may also intercept out-of-band communications like SMS or email to capture one-time codes. Notable incidents include Chimera registering alternate phone numbers to intercept SMS codes, Kimsuky using proprietary tools to capture one-time passwords, and LAPSUS$ replaying session tokens to trigger MFA prompts. Mitigation strategies include securing devices and services used for out-of-band communications, employing robust key management practices, and monitoring for suspicious activities related to MFA processes.
